ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Pancharangi Dal; mapped to the closest parent class 'lentil dish' (FOODON_03315091) as an approximate match. Pancharangi Dal is a five-lentil preparation not individually enumerated in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Lentils, cooked) used as the closest defensible anchor. Pancharangi Dal is a multi-legume spiced dish; no exact FoodEx2 entry exists for this preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Pancharangi Dal or a sufficiently close analogue in the clinical terminology system.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Pancharangi Dal is approximately 200 g (one bowl), typically consumed alongside rice or Indian breads such as roti or naan.

nutrient_source.title: Pancharangi Dal — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pancharangi Dal (cr_id G00011010), which is a canonical reviewed record with confidence 0.95. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this source. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record and is estimated from ICMR-NIN mixed dal reference values; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; mixed lentil / dal analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pancharangi Dal (five-lentil blend). GI estimated at 32 based on the University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked mixed lentils and split-pea dal preparations (GI range 22–38, mean ~32). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (32) × available carbohydrate per serving (12.33 g/100g × 200 g serving = 24.66 g) / 100 = 7.9 g. This is a low-GI, low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 383 mg/100g is in the borderline range (200–400 mg/100g); partially offset by potassium 292 mg/100g and dietary fibre 3.4 g/100g.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation to bring sodium below 300 mg/100g, limit portion to one bowl (200 g), and avoid pairing with other high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or papadums.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~32) and high dietary fibre (3.4 g/100g) slow postprandial glucose absorption; carbohydrate content is modest at 12.33 g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pancharangi Dal is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low GI and high fibre content; patients should control portion size to one bowl (200 g) and avoid pairing with large servings of white rice or refined bread to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary fibre (3.4 g/100g) and plant protein (3.68 g/100g) are cardioprotective; however, ghee-based tempering contributes saturated fat (~1.8 g/100g) and sodium is borderline elevated at 383 mg/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, Pancharangi Dal provides beneficial fibre and plant protein but ghee should be minimised or substituted with a small amount of olive oil, and sodium intake should be monitored by requesting reduced-salt preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Turmeric (curcumin) and ginger in the tempering provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols; dietary fibre supports gut microbiome immune modulation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pancharangi Dal is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile, but individuals sensitive to nightshades (tomato, chilli) should monitor their response and request preparation without these ingredients if needed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pancharangi Dal is a traditional North Indian mixed-lentil dish made from five varieties of lentils and legumes — typically chana dal (split chickpeas), masoor dal (red lentils), moong dal (split mung beans), urad dal (split black gram), and toor dal (split pigeon peas). The name 'Pancharangi' means 'five colours' in Hindi, reflecting the visual diversity of the five legumes. In restaurant preparations, the dal is slow-cooked overnight in a tandoor oven, which imparts a distinctive smoky depth and allows the legumes to fully soften and meld. It is finished with a house-special masala tadka (tempering) of ghee, cumin, onion, tomato, ginger, garlic, turmeric, coriander, and green chilli. The texture is thick and creamy with a hearty, earthy flavour balanced by aromatic spices. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 111 kcal, 3.68 g protein, 5.65 g fat, 12.33 g carbohydrate, 3.4 g dietary fibre, and 383 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 32 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The high fibre content supports gut health and cardiovascular protection. Sodium is borderline elevated due to added salt and masala; reduced-salt preparation is advisable for hypertensive individuals. In Singapore, Pancharangi Dal is served in Indian restaurants as a main accompaniment to rice or Indian breads at lunch and dinner. It is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains ghee (dairy), making it unsuitable for vegans.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database canonical reviewed record (cr_id G00011010, confidence 0.95). The record is marked as reviewed with provenance 'canonical'. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN mixed dal reference values as it was not reported in the HPB record, which slightly reduces certainty for that field only. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed lentil preparations.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods reference values for mixed cooked dal preparations, as the HPB record does not report saturated fat separately. This is an approximation and should be updated when a direct measurement becomes available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific five-lentil restaurant preparation is not available in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in the HPB record. 4. All vitamin fields (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg) are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this dish and no sufficiently close approved-source analogue with complete vitamin data was available. 5. phosphorus_mg and zinc_mg and selenium_mcg are set to -1 for the same reason. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is plausible for a plant-based lentil dish with minimal ghee. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0: ghee contains negligible industrially produced trans fats; naturally occurring ruminant trans fats in ghee are present at trace levels below reporting thresholds. 8. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked mixed lentils and split-pea dal (GI range 22–38); no direct GI measurement for Pancharangi Dal was found in the approved GI database. 9. Cuisine classified as Indian; this dish is served in Singapore Indian restaurants and is tagged with region SG. Multilingual names provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements.
